SECURITY AFFAIRS MALWARE NEWSLETTER ROUND 106
Security Affairs newsletter Round 586 by Pierluigi Paganini – INTERNATIONAL EDITION
Hackers abuse ViPNet software to target Russian govt agencies

executive_brief:
The last week has marked a dangerous inflection point in the 'AI Arms Race,' shifting from theoretical prompt injection to the deployment of AI-augmented botnets and automated vulnerability 'vending machines.' We are seeing a terrifying compression of the exploit window; attackers are now using LLMs to reverse-engineer patches in real-time and deploy functional exploits against critical infrastructure before organizations can even initiate their patch cycles. This is no longer about slow-moving APTs, but about AI-accelerated automation that can build C2 infrastructure in minutes and scan the global IPv4 space for specific flaws in hours.

Simultaneously, the 'Trust Boundary' has completely collapsed. We observed high-profile supply chain compromises in the npm ecosystem and the weaponization of 'trusted' developer tools like Cursor and GitHub. The most alarming trend is the emergence of 'ClickFix' social engineering—a high-conversion tactic that bypasses traditional security warnings by tricking users into executing malicious code directly in their terminals. For executives, the takeaway is clear: your traditional patch-and-perimeter model is mathematically unable to keep pace with AI-driven exploitation. Resilience now requires shifting toward identity-centric zero trust and aggressive, automated recovery validation.

threat_stories.headline: The AI-Accelerated Exploit Cycle: From 'Vending Machines' to Botnets
threat_stories.narrative:
The boundary between AI research and active weaponization has vanished. This week, researchers demonstrated a 'vulnerability vending machine' that uses LLMs to autonomously discover and exploit zero-days in WordPress plugins [179]. This is not an isolated experiment; it is mirroring real-world activity where the 'Patriot Bait' actor used AI to build and deploy a C2 botnet in just six minutes [140]. Even more concerning is the evolution of TuxBot v3, an IoT botnet whose development shows clear signs of LLM assistance, allowing it to target 17 different CPU architectures with a modular payload delivery system [143, 217].

This automation is creating a 'Patch Gap.' While Microsoft released a record-breaking 622 patches this month [108, 154], attackers are using AI to perform 'patch diffing'—analyzing the fix to instantly create an exploit for the vulnerability [231]. This was evident in the rapid exploitation of the SharePoint RCE flaws shortly after disclosure [269]. The result is a landscape where the time between a patch release and a functional exploit has shrunk to near zero.

Furthermore, AI agents themselves have become the attack vector. The 'ClaudeBleed' vulnerability demonstrates how rogue browser extensions can hijack the delegated privileges of AI assistants to read Gmail, Google Docs, and Calendar data without user interaction [112, 178]. We are seeing the birth of 'Agentic Malware'—code that doesn't just execute a script, but uses an LLM to navigate a victim's environment and make decisions on how to exfiltrate data most effectively [222].

timeline.event: Reports emerge of attackers using AI-generated PowerShell scripts for Active Directory mapping [14].
timeline.significance: Shows AI moving into the reconnaissance phase of the kill chain.

timeline.event: Patriot Bait actor deploys C2 botnet in 6 minutes using AI [140].
timeline.significance: Demonstrates extreme acceleration of infrastructure deployment.

timeline.event: Introduction of the AI 'Vulnerability Vending Machine' for WordPress [179].
timeline.significance: Proves AI can autonomously find and exploit zero-days.

timeline.event: TuxBot v3 revealed as an LLM-assisted IoT botnet [182, 217].
timeline.significance: Confirms AI is being used to write cross-platform malware at scale.

threat_stories.impact_assessment: Critical. Affects all organizations utilizing AI agents, WordPress installations, and those relying on traditional 30-day patch cycles.
threat_stories.action_required: Implement 'Virtual Patching' via WAF/IPS to block exploit patterns immediately upon disclosure. Audit all AI agent permissions (e.g., Claude, Gemini) and disable 'Act without asking' modes. Transition from periodic scanning to continuous exposure management.

threat_stories.headline: The ClickFix Epidemic: Weaponizing the Terminal
threat_stories.narrative:
A new and highly effective social engineering trend dubbed 'ClickFix' is bypassing the most sophisticated EDRs by targeting the one place users trust: their own terminal. Instead of delivering a malicious .exe, attackers use fake 'verification' pages (often mimicking Cloudflare) that instruct users to copy a string of text and paste it into their macOS or Windows terminal to 'fix' a problem [255].

This technique was the primary delivery mechanism for the 'ClickLock Stealer' and 'OkoBot' frameworks [130, 249]. Once the user pastes the command, the malware executes in the context of the user, bypassing Gatekeeper and other OS-level protections. ClickLock takes this further by repeatedly killing all visible applications every 210ms, effectively holding the user's computer hostage until they provide their login password, which is then stolen [249, 268].

This represents a shift toward 'human-in-the-loop' exploitation. By tricking the user into becoming the installer, the attacker avoids the need for a complex software vulnerability. The OkoBot framework then deploys specialized implants like 'SeedHunter' to phish for Trezor and Ledger seed phrases, showing a clear focus on high-value cryptocurrency theft [130, 188].

timeline.event: First sightings of ClickLock Stealer using ClickFix lures [255].
timeline.significance: Start of the campaign to weaponize terminal pasting.

timeline.event: OkoBot framework identified using ClickFix to deploy 20 different payloads [259].
timeline.significance: Shows the scalability of the ClickFix delivery method.

timeline.event: ClickLock observed killing apps every 210ms to force password entry [249].
timeline.significance: Introduction of aggressive psychological pressure to force credential disclosure.

threat_stories.impact_assessment: High. Particularly dangerous for developers, crypto-asset holders, and macOS users who are accustomed to using the terminal.
threat_stories.action_required: Deploy EDR rules to alert on suspicious shell script execution (e.g., curl | sh patterns). Implement 'Terminal' access restrictions for non-admin users. Conduct urgent user awareness training specifically targeting the 'paste into terminal' lure.

threat_stories.headline: Critical Infrastructure Under Siege: The Russian Router Campaign
threat_stories.narrative:
A coordinated campaign by Russian state-sponsored actors, including FSB Center 16, is targeting the very foundation of network trust: the router. By exploiting legacy vulnerabilities and poor hygiene, these actors are establishing deep persistence within critical infrastructure across the US, UK, and EU [19, 103, 173].

The campaign leverages a 'low-tech' but highly effective approach. Attackers scan for internet-exposed SNMP agents using default community strings (SNMPv1/v2) to exfiltrate device configurations [39, 73]. They also exploit the 'Cisco Smart Install' feature and a decade-old RCE flaw (CVE-2008-4128) that remains active on unpatched legacy hardware [55, 173].

This is not merely about data theft; it is about operational preparation of the environment. By compromising the routing layer, the FSB can bypass perimeter defenses, manipulate traffic, and maintain a silent foothold for future sabotage. The severity is highlighted by the joint UK/EU sanctions following a direct attack on the Polish power grid [19, 34, 39].

timeline.event: UK and EU impose joint sanctions over the Poland power grid attack [19].
timeline.significance: Direct evidence of router-level compromise leading to physical infrastructure impact.

timeline.event: CISA adds legacy Cisco IOS flaw (CVE-2008-4128) to KEV catalog [55].
timeline.significance: Formal recognition that 18-year-old bugs are still primary vectors for state actors.

timeline.event: Joint US/Allied advisory warns of Russian APTs targeting critical routers [173, 103].
timeline.significance: Urgent call for global router hygiene to prevent systemic infrastructure failure.

threat_stories.impact_assessment: Critical. Affects energy, communications, and defense sectors globally, specifically those running legacy Cisco hardware.
threat_stories.action_required: Immediately disable SNMPv1/v2 and migrate to SNMPv3 with authPriv. Disable Cisco Smart Install on all devices. Retire all end-of-life (EoL) routers running IOS 12.4 or older. Implement strict ACLs to restrict management interface access to trusted IPs only.

actor_profiles.name: ShinyHunters
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Conducting a year-long campaign targeting Salesforce environments by abusing OAuth trust relationships and third-party integrations to exfiltrate corporate data [61, 76].

actor_profiles.name: Scattered Spider
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Executing high-impact social engineering attacks, including a massive breach at Qantas via tech support scams [193] and a £29 million hack of Transport for London (TfL) [228, 245].

actor_profiles.name: UAT-11795
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Distributing trojanized installers for Zoom and Webex to deploy the Starland RAT, featuring a novel blockchain-anchored C2 fallback via Polygon smart contracts [226, 285].

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Critical deserialization vulnerability allowing unauthenticated remote code execution (RCE).
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Apply July 2026 Patch Tuesday updates immediately.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) leading to unauthenticated remote command execution.
Improper input validation allowing unauthenticated account takeover via network access.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Update Zoom Desktop Client to version 7.0.0 or later.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Out-of-bounds write flaw leading to memory corruption and potential RCE.

hunting_leads.title: Hunt for ClickFix Terminal Execution
hunting_leads.context: Detect users pasting malicious scripts into the terminal to install stealer malware like OkoBot or ClickLock.
hunting_leads.query: index=endpoint_logs process_name=bash OR process_name=zsh OR process_name=powershell.exe | search cmd="*curl*" AND cmd="*| sh*" OR cmd="*Invoke-WebRequest*" AND cmd="*iex*"

hunting_leads.title: Detecting Cursor IDE Malicious git.exe
hunting_leads.context: The Cursor IDE automatically executes git.exe in the project root. Hunt for git.exe processes spawning from unusual project directories.
hunting_leads.query: index=endpoint_logs process_name=git.exe | where NOT (parent_process_name="cursor.exe" AND path="C:\\Program Files\\Git\\bin\\git.exe")

statistics.key_changes: Exploit window has compressed from weeks to minutes due to AI-assisted patch diffing and C2 deployment.
emerging_trends: Agentic Malware: AI agents being used as active pivots within networks
ClickFix: High-conversion social engineering bypassing OS security via terminal pasting
Blockchain C2: Using smart contracts (e.g., Polygon) for immutable fallback C2 addresses
declining_threats: Traditional .exe/macro-based phishing (replaced by living-off-the-land terminal commands)
